narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, you've shown determination in pursuing your dream, and we appreciate the courage it takes to put yourself out there with your undergraduate GPA of 3.10 and LSAT score of 28. Your family comes from a middle-income background, and you're planning to attend full-time, which is the path most students take. Unfortunately, the admissions model predicted a 71.1% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first try, which is why your application was rejected—schools use this to prioritize applicants likely to succeed and pass the bar right away. The biggest factor pulling the prediction toward failure was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 1, meaning your grades that year placed you in the bottom tenth of your peers, far below the typical student's middle-of-the-pack standing. Your Year 1 decile of 1 also played a significant role in the same direction, signaling early academic struggles compared to the average applicant. On a brighter note, your undergraduate GPA actually nudged the prediction slightly toward passing, as it's close to the dataset average and shows solid college performance. Your LSAT score and full-time status had smaller positive influences too, aligning reasonably with what succeeds for most students. Overall, your lower high school deciles stood out as the most influential red flags when compared to typical admitted students who rank higher in their classes. This doesn't define your potential—many overcome early hurdles to excel in law. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score, strengthening your recent academic record through additional coursework or a post-bac program, and highlighting any leadership or community work that showcases your resilience as a Black woman in this field. Reapply after addressing these areas, and seek mentorship from alumni who've succeeded despite similar starts. You've got this—keep pushing.
